memes.label: A Child
memes.description: Children encountered by the unnamed narrator in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, whose innocence and vulnerability mirror the narrator's own while highlighting his social exclusion.

memes.label: A Police Officer
memes.description: A police officer encountered by the unnamed narrator in Hunger, representing the authority and social order that the narrator both fears and, in moments of desperation, appeals to.

memes.label: A Wanderer Plays on Muted Strings
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1909 novel, part of the Pan cycle — a late entry in his prolific 1897–1912 period, published the year after Benoni and Rosa.
aliases: A Wanderer Plays with Muted Strings

memes.label: Aftenpost
memes.description: The Norwegian newspaper Aftenposten, read by the narrator of Hunger on Grændsen, representing the public world of print and information from which his poverty excludes him.

memes.label: Akershus Fortress
memes.description: "A medieval castle and fortress overlooking the Kristiania harbour; a landmark visible from much of the city in Hamsun's Hunger."

memes.label: Alexander Kielland
memes.description: "Norwegian novelist and short story writer (1849–1906), one of the 'Four Greats' of Norwegian literature alongside Ibsen, Bjørnson, and Lie."

memes.label: "Alfred A. Knopf, Inc."
memes.description: "American publishing house founded in 1915; published the 1921 English edition of Hunger with Edwin Björkman's introduction, bringing Hamsun to American readers."

memes.label: Alienation
memes.description: "The condition of estrangement from society, self, or meaning; a central thematic concern of Hunger and a foundational concept in modernist literature."

memes.label: "Allegory of a Conflagration in a Bookshop"
memes.description: "An allegorical newspaper piece the narrator works on throughout Part II, depicting burning brains rather than burning books, intended as payment to the Commandor."

memes.label: "Anxiety"
memes.description: "The narrator's pervasive state of nervous unease, manifesting as irritability and heightened sensitivity in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: The Artist
memes.description: The concept of the artist as a distinct psychological and social type in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, embodied by the unnamed narrator whose identity as a writer is both his greatest claim to dignity and the source of his suffering.

memes.label: Artistic Creation
memes.description: "The process of artistic creation as explored in Hunger, where the narrator's writing — from feuilletons to philosophical fragments — is both a survival strategy and a mode of self-definition."

memes.label: "At the Gates of the Kingdom"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1895 drama, the opening play of a trilogy featuring the character Ivar Kareno"

memes.label: At the Mercy of Life
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1910 play At the Mercy of Life, listed among his works in the preface to Hunger.

memes.label: August Strindberg
memes.description: Swedish playwright and novelist (1849–1912), named alongside Ibsen in the preface of Knut Hamsun's Hunger as one of the Scandinavian literary giants whose era was succeeded by Hamsun's generation.

memes.label: "Begging"
memes.description: "The act of soliciting money or food from strangers, a recurring source of humiliation and moral struggle for the narrator in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Beneath the Autumn Star
memes.description: A novel by Knut Hamsun published in 1906, preceded by the travel sketches Stridende Liv (1905) and followed by Benoni (1908), forming part of Hamsun's mature period of novel-writing.

memes.label: Benoni
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1908 novel — paired with Rosa as sequels to Pan, forming the first stage of Hamsun's shift toward a wider social canvas.

memes.label: Benoni and Rosa
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's paired novels Benoni and Rosa (both 1908), forming a diptych that traces the widening horizon of Hamsun's social vision beyond the solitary consciousness of his early work.

memes.label: Bergen
memes.description: "Major Norwegian coastal city; the historical trading center from which many 19th-century Norwegian emigrants departed, including some of Hamsun's contemporaries."

memes.label: "Bernt Akers Street"
memes.description: "A street in Christiania where the theological student Hans Pauli Pettersen lived at No. 10, appearing in Part II — the destination of an exhausting journey that ends in crushing disappointment."

memes.label: Bjørnstjerne Bjørnson
memes.description: "Norwegian writer, Nobel laureate (1903), and contemporary of Henrik Ibsen; mentioned in Edwin Björkman's introduction to Hunger as a Scandinavian literary master."

memes.label: Bodily Experience
memes.description: Index of pages exploring bodily states, physical sensations, and somatic experience in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: "Body"
memes.description: "The narrator's relationship with his own body — a site of suffering, shame, and estrangement — in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Bogstadveien
memes.description: "A street in Kristiania (Oslo) running through the western districts; part of the urban geography of Hamsun's Hunger."

memes.label: Bookseller Pascha
memes.description: A Christiania bookseller whose shop the narrator passes and uses as a landmark in his wanderings through the city.

memes.label: Boredom and Stimulation
memes.description: "The oscillation between boredom and overstimulation that characterizes the narrator's experience in Hunger, reflecting the sensory overload of modern urban life."

memes.label: "Brushwood"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1903 short story collection, Kratskog, published during a prolific middle period alongside fiction, travel writing, and poetry"

memes.label: Camilla Collett
memes.description: "Norwegian novelist and feminist (1813–1895), author of 'The District Governor's Daughters' (1854–55); Wergeland's sister and an early voice for women's emancipation in Norwegian literature."

memes.label: Carl Johann Street
memes.description: The main boulevard of Christiania (now Oslo), a frequent setting for the narrator's walks and encounters in Hunger.

memes.label: Caucasus
memes.description: "Mountain region between the Black and Caspian Seas; Hamsun traveled there in 1899 and published the travel sketches 'In Wonderland' (1903)."

memes.label: Chance
memes.description: The role of chance, luck, and random encounters in shaping the protagonist's fate in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Index of Characters
memes.description: Alphabetical index of all named and unnamed characters appearing in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Chicago
memes.description: Major American city where Knut Hamsun worked as a streetcar conductor during his second American visit (1886–1888); the urban counterpart to his North Dakota frontier experience.

memes.label: Children of the Age
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1913 novel that marked a new direction in his career — his vision turning social for the first time, set around Segelfoss manor.

memes.label: "Children of the Time"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1913 novel, a social critique of modern capitalism set in Segelfoss"

memes.label: Christiania
memes.description: The Norwegian capital city (now Oslo) in which Hunger is set — both physical backdrop and symbolic arena of social judgment, conformity, and the narrator's isolation.

memes.label: Places in Christiania
memes.description: Index of locations in Christiania (now Oslo) that appear in Knut Hamsun's Hunger — streets, buildings, parks, and landmarks.

memes.label: "Renaming of Christiania to Kristiania"
memes.description: "The 1877 renaming of Norway's capital from Christiania to Kristiania, later renamed Oslo in 1925"

memes.label: Christy the Grocer
memes.description: A grocer at No. 13 Groenlandsleret in Christiania to whom the narrator applies for a book-keeping position but never personally follows up — a minor figure who represents one of the narrator's many missed opportunities for stable employment.

memes.label: "Cisler's Music Shop"
memes.description: "A music shop in Christiania owned by Cisler, where the narrator stops to observe Ylajali in Part I and whose proprietor he later invokes as proof of his sanity in Part II."

memes.label: Class and Status
memes.description: "The social hierarchy and status distinctions that structure the world of Hunger, where the narrator's fall from bourgeois respectability is a central source of his suffering."

memes.label: "Compassion"
memes.description: "The narrator's complex relationship with the compassion of others — something he simultaneously seeks, manipulates, and resents — in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Concepts in Hunger
memes.description: Index of abstract concepts, themes, and motifs that structure the psychological and philosophical dimensions of Hunger.

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The narrator's internal moral faculty in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, which alternates between tormenting self-reproach and being silenced by the imperatives of survival.

memes.label: Copenhagen
memes.description: Capital of Denmark; the literary center where Hamsun first published the sketch of Hunger in the Danish periodical New Earth in 1888.

memes.label: Creative Expression
memes.description: Index of pages exploring creativity, artistic expression, literary technique, and aesthetic dimensions in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Creativity
memes.description: The narrator's struggle to produce writing under conditions of starvation and mental instability in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, where creativity oscillates between manic inspiration and paralyzing blockage.

memes.label: Crimes of Futurity
memes.description: A fictional article and thesis that the unnamed narrator of Knut Hamsun's Hunger attempts to write while starving in Christiania, representing both his intellectual ambition and the gulf between his aspirations and reality.

memes.label: Dagbladet
memes.description: Norwegian daily newspaper based in Christiania, where the narrator of Hunger seeks to publish his writings.

memes.label: Death
memes.description: The concept of death in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, which appears as both a literal possibility (the narrator's hunger could kill him) and a metaphorical presence that haunts his consciousness and shapes his relationship to life.

memes.label: Delirium
memes.description: The state of delirium experienced by the unnamed narrator in Knut Hamsun's Hunger — a compound of starvation-induced hallucination, feverish creativity, and psychological fragmentation that drives the novel's most stylistically radical passages.

memes.label: "Delusion"
memes.description: "The narrator's tendency toward grandiose self-deception and distorted reality perception, intensified by starvation in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Despair
memes.description: A profound emotional state experienced by the unnamed narrator in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, marked by episodes of acute hopelessness alternating with manic creative energy.

memes.label: Dignity
memes.description: The narrator's persistent, often absurd claim to dignity in Knut Hamsun's Hunger — a psychological defense mechanism that survives starvation, homelessness, and social degradation, and that drives many of the novel's most characteristic scenes.

memes.label: Drammen
memes.description: A port city southwest of Oslo; referenced in Hunger as part of the wider Norwegian geography beyond Kristiania.

memes.label: Dreamers
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1904 novel, published in the same year as The Wild Choir, representing his middle-period fiction between the early psychological novels and the later epic works.

memes.label: Dreams
memes.description: The narrator's visionary and escapist mental states in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, ranging from ecstatic creative visions to desperate fantasies of rescue, and closely tied to his fever and hunger.

memes.label: Editor Lynge
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1893 novel, paired with New Earth as a social study of Christiania's Bohemia, published between Mysteries and Pan in Hamsun's early career.

memes.label: Edwin Björkman
memes.description: Author of the critical-biographical preface to the English edition of Knut Hamsun's Hunger, which provides the literary-historical framework for understanding the novel's significance.

memes.label: Eline Kareno
memes.description: "Fictional character in Hamsun's dramatic trilogy; wife of Ivar Kareno throughout 'At the Gates of the Kingdom,' 'The Game of Life,' and 'Sunset Glow.'"

memes.label: Events in Hunger
memes.description: Chronological and thematic index of historical and narrative events referenced in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: A pervasive emotional state in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, experienced by the unnamed narrator as anxiety about social judgment, physical survival, and the loss of creative identity.

memes.label: Featured Content
memes.description: Curated index of featured pages in the Hunger knowledge base — the highest-quality entries across all types.

memes.label: Fever
memes.description: A recurring physiological and metaphorical state in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, blurring the boundary between the narrator's physical starvation and his psychological instability.

memes.label: Fictoria
memes.description: "A typographical error: Björkman writes 'Fictoria' for Victoria, Hamsun's 1898 love story. This page documents the bibliographic variant. Redirect to [[victoria]]."

memes.label: Friedrich Nietzsche
memes.description: "German philosopher whose ideas on individualism and the Übermensch influenced literary modernism; Björkman describes Hamsun's hero Ivar Kareno as 'a sort of Nietzschean rebel.'"

memes.label: Frogner
memes.description: "A residential district of Kristiania, representing the prosperous western quarters far from the narrator's impoverished Vaterland haunts in Hunger."

memes.label: Fyodor Dostoyevski
memes.description: Russian novelist (1821–1881), cited in the preface of Knut Hamsun's Hunger as a major influence on Hamsun's work and as a point of comparison for his psychological method.

memes.label: Gender and Desire
memes.description: "The role of gender relations and erotic desire in Hunger, particularly the narrator's encounter with Ylajali and his construction of femininity as both salvation and threat."

memes.label: George Egerton
memes.description: Translator of the English edition of Knut Hamsun's Hunger, whose 1899 version brought the novel to English-speaking readers and is the source text for this wiki's corpus.

memes.label: Grace
memes.description: The concept of grace in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, operating on theological, social, and personal levels as the narrator alternately hopes for divine mercy and experiences the humiliation of having fallen from social favor.

memes.label: Grændsen
memes.description: A commercial street in Christiania where the narrator of Hunger conducts his urban wandering, encounters acquaintances, and pawns his possessions — a theater of social visibility and economic transaction.

memes.label: Groenlandsleret
memes.description: A street in Christiania where grocer Christy's shop is located, and where the narrator seeks book-keeping employment.

memes.label: Growth of the Soil
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1917 Nobel Prize-winning novel (Markens Grøde), the culmination of his mature period celebrating primitive life and man's relationship with the soil — referenced in the preface to Hunger as the destination of Hamsun's literary trajectory.

memes.label: Guilt
memes.description: Guilt as a psychological torment — both real and imagined — in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Gyldendal Norsk Forlag
memes.description: Norway's leading publishing house, founded in 1925 from the Danish Gyldendal, which published Knut Hamsun's collected works and remains the primary publisher of Scandinavian literature.

memes.label: "Hamsun's Visits to America"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's two visits to the United States in the 1880s, working in North Dakota and Chicago; formative experiences that shaped his literary worldview."

memes.label: Hamsun Nobel Prize
memes.description: Knut Hamsun was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1920 for his novel "Growth of the Soil" (Markens Grøde), capping a literary career that began with Hunger thirty years earlier.

memes.label: Hans Pauli
memes.description: A recurring character in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, a peasant-farmer acquaintance of the unnamed narrator who provides occasional material support and serves as a measure of the narrator's declining social status.

memes.label: Henrik Ibsen
memes.description: Norwegian playwright (1828–1906), referred to in the preface of Knut Hamsun's Hunger as the towering figure of Scandinavian literature whose death marked the transition to a new literary generation.

relations.label: At the Gates of the Kingdom

memes.label: Henrik Pontoppidan
memes.description: "Danish realist writer and Nobel laureate (1917, shared with Karl Gjellerup); mentioned in Björkman's introduction as one of the leading Scandinavian writers of Hamsun's era."

memes.label: Henrik Wergeland
memes.description: "Norwegian poet and nationalist (1808–1845), a foundational figure in Norwegian literary identity whose romantic nationalism preceded Hamsun's generation."

memes.label: Homansbyen
memes.description: "A residential district in Kristiania, developed in the 1860s–70s; represents the expanding bourgeois neighborhoods that contrast with the narrator's impoverished quarters in Hunger."

memes.label: Hope
memes.description: The role of hope as a sustaining but often deluded psychological force in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Humiliation
memes.description: The experience of humiliation as a recurring psychological state in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, arising from poverty, social rejection, and the narrator's acute self-consciousness, and functioning as a primary driver of his erratic behavior.

memes.label: "Hunger"
memes.description: "The physical sensation of starvation and its psychological effects, serving as the central driving force and metaphor in Knut Hamsun's novel Hunger"

memes.label: Hunger as Metaphor
memes.description: "The multi-layered symbolic function of hunger in Hamsun's novel — simultaneously literal starvation, creative appetite, spiritual yearning, and existential condition."

memes.label: "Imagination"
memes.description: "The narrator's creative faculty and its turbulent relationship with starvation, serving as both his professional resource and a source of psychological instability in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: In Wonderland
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1903 travel-sketches In Wonderland, listed among his works in the preface to Hunger.

memes.label: Individualism
memes.description: "The ethical and aesthetic stance prioritizing individual will and authenticity over social convention; a defining value of Hamsun's literary worldview and his Nietzschean heroes."

memes.label: "Inspiration"
memes.description: "The narrator's experience of creative inspiration as a heightened state bordering on mania in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: "Isolation"
memes.description: "The narrator's physical and psychological separation from society, a defining condition of his existence in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Ivar Kareno
memes.description: Fictional protagonist of Knut Hamsun's dramatic trilogy, a student and thinker who evolves from rebel to establishment figure across three plays.

memes.label: Johann Arendt Happolati
memes.description: A fictional mathematician invented by the narrator, described as having discovered a new mathematical system — a product of the protagonist's hunger-driven imagination.

memes.label: Jonas Lie
memes.description: "Norwegian novelist (1833–1908), one of the 'Four Greats' of Norwegian literature; his novels of family life and the sea established Norwegian prose fiction."

memes.label: Karl Johans Gate
memes.description: "The principal street of Kristiania (Oslo), named after King Karl Johan of Sweden-Norway; a frequent setting in Hunger where the narrator wanders and observes city life."

memes.label: "Joachim Kierulf"
memes.description: "A wool-dealer in Christiania whom the narrator invents during a delusional episode in Part III of Hunger — a phantom figure who reveals the narrator's starvation-induced confabulation and the collapse of memory into fantasy."

memes.label: Knut Hamsun
memes.description: Norwegian author (1859–1952), author of Hunger (1890), Nobel Prize laureate (1920), and one of the foremost figures of Scandinavian modernism.

relations.label: The Spiritual Life of Modern America

memes.label: Kristiania
memes.description: "The capital city of Norway, called Christiania from 1624 to 1925 (now Oslo); the primary setting of Knut Hamsun's Hunger."

memes.label: Kristiania Fjord
memes.description: "The fjord on which Kristiania (Oslo) is situated; the harbour and docks of Hunger open onto this body of water, symbolizing connection to the wider world."

memes.label: Leo Tolstoy
memes.description: "Russian novelist and moral philosopher; Björkman notes that Hamsun was spoken of in Russia as 'the equal of Tolstoy and Dostoyevski,' indicating Hamsun's high reputation there."

memes.label: Lieutenant Glahn
memes.description: "The protagonist of Knut Hamsun's novel Pan (1894); a solitary hunter living in the Northland whose primitively spontaneous reactions to nature embody a central Hamsun archetype."

memes.label: Literary Impressionism
memes.description: "A literary mode emphasizing subjective perception and momentary impressions over objective description; Björkman compares Hamsun's method in Hunger to 'the Impressionistic temper of a Gauguin or Cezanne.'"

memes.label: Lofoten
memes.description: Archipelago in northern Norway above the Arctic Circle, where Knut Hamsun's family moved when he was four years old and whose dramatic landscape influenced his literary imagination.

memes.label: Love
memes.description: The role of romantic and passionate love as a driving human emotion in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Madness
memes.description: A recurring psychological state in Hunger — the narrator's descent into delirium, hallucination, and mental fragmentation under the pressure of starvation and social isolation.

memes.label: Mark Twain
memes.description: "American writer and humorist; Björkman notes that Hamsun's novel Mysteries blends 'the spirits of Dostoyevski and Mark Twain,' highlighting its tonal range from tragedy to comedy."

memes.label: Memory
memes.description: The faculty of recollection in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, which the narrator alternately treasures as a connection to his past self and experiences as a source of painful contrast with his present degradation.

memes.label: Miss Andersen
memes.description: Proprietor in Christiania whose advertisement for "winding-sheets" is visible from the narrator's attic window.

memes.label: Missy
memes.description: Recurring acquaintance of the narrator in Hunger, who gossips about Ylajali and the Duke, and shows gossipy interest in the narrator's affairs.

memes.label: Modernism
memes.description: "The broad artistic and literary movement of the late 19th and early 20th centuries characterized by formal experimentation, subjective consciousness, and a break with tradition; Hunger is among its foundational texts."

memes.label: Möller Street
memes.description: A street in Christiania known for its eating-house, where the narrator pauses outside in hunger unable to afford even the cheapest meal — symbol of the city's exclusionary abundance.

memes.label: Morgenbladet
memes.description: The Norwegian newspaper Morgenbladet — whose old issues paper the narrator's rented room in Hunger's opening scene, symbolizing the world of print from which he is excluded.

memes.label: Morgenbladet
memes.description: "Norwegian daily newspaper founded in 1819; old issues of Morgenbladet serve as wallpaper in the narrator's attic room in Hunger, symbolizing his immersion in textual production."

memes.label: Mortality and Decay
memes.description: "The thematic preoccupation with death, bodily decay, and impermanence that pervades Hunger, from the narrator's physical deterioration to his cemetery meditations."

memes.label: Mother Monsen
memes.description: "A maternal figure in Hunger who offers the narrator lodging or food; represents the possibility of care that the narrator's pride prevents him from accepting."

memes.label: Mysteries
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1892 novel, the immediate successor to Hunger, described as a psychological novel that deepened Hamsun's exploration of abnormal mental states.

memes.label: Narrative Unreliability
memes.description: "A narrative mode where the narrator's account cannot be taken at face value; Hunger employs unreliability through the distorting lens of the narrator's hunger, pride, and creative imagination."

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: The concept of nature in Knut Hamsun's Hunger and its broader literary context, functioning as a shaping force on character, a source of creative inspiration, and a setting that dwarfs human concerns.

memes.label: Neo-Romanticism
memes.description: "Late 19th-century literary movement in Scandinavia emphasizing subjectivity, nature mysticism, and rejection of naturalist determinism; Hamsun was hailed as one of its 'chief heralds.'"

memes.label: New Earth
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1893 novel (Ny Jord), a social study of Christiania's Bohemia, published the same year as Editor Lynge.

memes.label: New Earth
memes.description: The Danish literary periodical Ny Jord (New Earth) in which the first sketch of Knut Hamsun's Hunger appeared in 1888, marking the novel's initial public emergence and Hamsun's breakthrough into Scandinavian literary consciousness.

memes.label: Newspapers and Periodicals
memes.description: Index of newspapers, periodicals, and serial publications referenced in Hunger and its critical context.

memes.label: North Dakota
memes.description: Northern US state where Knut Hamsun worked as a farmhand during his first visit to America in the 1880s; the frontier landscape shaped his critique of American civilization.

memes.label: Norway
memes.description: Nordic country in Northern Europe, the setting of Knut Hamsun's Hunger and the author's homeland, which shaped the novel's social, economic, and cultural context.

memes.label: Norway-Sweden Union
memes.description: "The political union between Norway and Sweden (1814–1905), forming the historical backdrop to late-19th-century Norwegian cultural nationalism relevant to Hamsun's era."

memes.label: "On Overgrown Paths"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1949 memoir, written at age 90 while under house arrest after World War II"

memes.label: Our Saviour's Cemetery
memes.description: The churchyard of Our Saviour's Church in Christiania, where the narrator of Hunger finds moments of quiet refuge among the graves — a space of death that paradoxically offers respite from the death-in-life of his hunger.

memes.label: "Our Saviour's Church"
memes.description: "A landmark church in Christiania whose clock tower serves as a recurring temporal reference point throughout Hunger — an impersonal, mechanical witness to the narrator's disintegration."

memes.label: Pan
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1894 novel -- one of his most celebrated works -- featuring Lieutenant Glahn in a lyrical story of nature, love, and psychological intensity set in the Norwegian Northland.

memes.label: Paul Cézanne
memes.description: "French Post-Impressionist painter; invoked by Björkman alongside Gauguin as an artistic parallel to Hamsun's impressionistic literary technique in Hunger."

memes.label: Paul Gauguin
memes.description: "French Post-Impressionist painter; Björkman invokes Gauguin and Cézanne as artistic analogies for Hamsun's impressionistic literary method in Hunger."

memes.label: Peer Gynt
memes.description: "Henrik Ibsen's 1867 dramatic poem; Björkman compares Hamsun's monk Vendt to Peer Gynt as a fellow dreamer-rebel against the universe."

memes.label: Philosophical Cognition
memes.description: A fictional three-volume philosophical treatise that the unnamed narrator of Hunger claims to be writing, representing his most grandiose intellectual ambition and the culminating self-deception of his starving period in Christiania.

memes.label: Philosophical Inquiry
memes.description: Index of pages exploring philosophical, existential, and religious dimensions of Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Places in Hunger
memes.description: Index of all locations — real and fictional — that appear in Hunger, from Christiania's streets to the rooms the protagonist inhabits.

memes.label: Poverty
memes.description: The material condition of extreme deprivation experienced by the unnamed narrator in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, functioning as both the novel's literal premise and a metaphor for spiritual and creative crisis.

memes.label: Pride
memes.description: A central psychological trait of the unnamed narrator in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, manifesting as defiant self-regard that paradoxically coexists with and often intensifies his suffering.

memes.label: Pride and Poverty
memes.description: "The central social-psychological dynamic in Hunger: the narrator's pride compels him to refuse help even as poverty destroys him, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of suffering."

memes.label: Prison
memes.description: The motif of imprisonment and confinement in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, operating as both a literal experience — the narrator's night in the guard-house — and a pervasive metaphor for his social, economic, and psychological condition.

memes.label: Project Gutenberg
memes.description: "The oldest digital library, founded in 1971; the English text of Hunger is distributed as a Project Gutenberg eBook (#8387), from which the Penguin/Knopf print editions derive."

memes.label: Prominent Entities
memes.description: Index of the most-referenced entities in the Hunger knowledge base — persons, places, concepts, and works with the strongest citation networks.

memes.label: Psychogeography
memes.description: The study of how geographical environment affects emotions and behavior; Hunger maps Kristiania as a psychological landscape where urban space and mental state are inseparable.

memes.label: Psychological Realism
memes.description: A mode of fiction that prioritizes internal psychological states over external action; Hunger exemplifies a radicalized psychological realism that anticipates stream-of-consciousness technique.

memes.label: Psychological Themes
memes.description: Index of pages exploring the psychological and mental dimensions of Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Publication of Hunger (1890)
memes.description: "The publication of Knut Hamsun's completed novel Hunger (Sult) in 1890, marking his literary breakthrough and a pivotal moment in the emergence of literary modernism."

memes.label: "Pyle Street"
memes.description: "A street in Christiania known to the narrator for its pawnbroker's shop — a recurring destination where he trades his last possessions for survival money across Parts I and II of Hunger."

memes.label: Queen Tamara
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1903 play, one of three subsequent plays written after the success of the Kareno trilogy's opening drama, representing Hamsun's sustained engagement with theatrical form.

memes.label: "Reality"
memes.description: "The tension between the narrator's inner world and external reality in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, where objective circumstances constantly intrude upon his fantasies and self-deceptions"

memes.label: Religion and Doubt
memes.description: "The role of religious thought and skepticism in Hunger, where biblical references and theological language coexist with the narrator's evident alienation from institutional faith."

memes.label: Rosa
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1908 novel — companion to Benoni, forming a two-part sequel to Pan and marking Hamsun's transition toward social fiction.

memes.label: Scandinavian Literature
memes.description: "The literary tradition of Norway, Sweden, and Denmark; the cultural context within which Hamsun wrote and against which Björkman situates Hunger's significance."

memes.label: Scandinavian Modern Breakthrough
memes.description: "The literary movement of the 1870s–1890s in Scandinavia, led by Georg Brandes, demanding that literature address contemporary social issues; the context from which Hamsun's modernism emerged."

memes.label: Scissors
memes.description: The newspaper office clerk and gatekeeper in Hunger, nicknamed "Scissors" by the narrator for his habit of clipping provincial papers.

memes.label: Segelfoss Manor
memes.description: "Fictional manor in Hamsun's later novels (Children of the Time, Segelfoss Town); represents the transition from agrarian to modern life that characterizes Hamsun's mature fiction."

memes.label: Segelfoss Town
memes.description: A novel by Knut Hamsun published in 1915, preceded by the broader Segelfoss series and followed by The Growth of the Soil (1917), marking Hamsun's later mature period.

memes.label: Selma Lagerlöf
memes.description: "Swedish author and the first woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature (1909); mentioned in Björkman's introduction as a leading Scandinavian writer contemporary with Hamsun."

memes.label: "Semb's Shop"
memes.description: "A shop in Christiania where the narrator buys cloth for a suit — or rather, uses the purchase as a pretext to wrap his bread in paper."

memes.label: Shallow Soil
memes.description: A novel by Knut Hamsun, grouped with Hunger and Fictoria in the English preface's list of Hamsun's early works, representing his initial period of novel-writing in the 1890s.

memes.label: Shame
memes.description: A pervasive psychological state experienced by the unnamed narrator of Hunger — social anxiety, self-consciousness, and moral torment amid desperate poverty.

memes.label: "Siesta"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1897 short story collection, containing the tender love story that prefigures Victoria"

memes.label: Social Dimensions
memes.description: Index of pages exploring social class, poverty, and economic dynamics in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Soul
memes.description: The narrator's conception of the soul in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, an ambiguous entity that is alternately a site of suffering, a source of creative energy, and a theological concept in crisis.

memes.label: Spirit
memes.description: The narrator's fluctuating inner state in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, encompassing both his creative vitality and his susceptibility to despair — a barometer of his psychological condition that rises and falls with his physical circumstances.

memes.label: St. Olav's Place
memes.description: A public square in Christiania — now Oslo — that recurs throughout Hunger as a site of the narrator's wandering, surveillance of Ylajali, and existential reflection.

memes.label: Stiftsgaarden
memes.description: A prominent building in Christiania used for official functions, referenced in the novel as a seat of status and authority.

memes.label: Stream of Consciousness
memes.description: "A narrative technique rendering the flow of thoughts, feelings, and sensations in a character's mind; Hunger's intense subjective narration anticipates this major modernist innovation."

memes.label: "Strength"
memes.description: "The narrator's fluctuating physical and mental fortitude — a resource he summons, loses, and desperately tries to regain — in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Struggle
memes.description: The theme of persistent striving against adversity — both external and internal — in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.

memes.label: Struggling Life
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1905 short-stories and travelling sketches Struggling Life, listed among his works in the preface to Hunger.

memes.label: "Suffering"
memes.description: "The narrator's experience of physical and psychological pain as a consequence of starvation in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Sunset Glow
memes.description: A play by Knut Hamsun published in 1898, following The Game of Life (1896) and completing Hamsun's mid-1890s dramatic period.

memes.label: Teresita
memes.description: "Fictional character in Hamsun's dramatic trilogy; one of Hamsun's 'many feminine embodiments of the passionate and changeable Northland nature,' alongside Eline in Kareno's life."

memes.label: The Attic
memes.description: The attic room at Bernt Akers Street No. 10, the narrator's second residence in Hunger, representing downward social mobility and deepening isolation.

memes.label: The Baker
memes.description: A baker encountered by the unnamed narrator in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, whose shop represents the tantalizing proximity of food that the narrator cannot afford — a recurring motif of desire and deprivation.

memes.label: The Bazaar
memes.description: The covered market arcade in Christiania where the narrator of Hunger pawns possessions, begs for food, and confronts the material logic of his poverty.

memes.label: The Beggar
memes.description: An unnamed beggar encountered by the narrator on the streets of Christiania, representing the social abyss the protagonist fears falling into.

memes.label: "The Bible"
memes.description: "The Christian Bible, a pervasive intertextual presence in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: The Blind Man
memes.description: "An unnamed blind man encountered by the narrator; exemplifies the novel's use of damaged, marginalized figures as mirrors for the protagonist's condition."

memes.label: The Butcher Woman
memes.description: A minor character in Hunger whom the narrator encounters in the streets of Kristiania; represents the anonymous urban encounters that populate the novel.

memes.label: The Cemetery
memes.description: A churchyard cemetery in Kristiania where the narrator of Hunger seeks refuge and contemplates mortality; a key symbolic location in the novel.

memes.label: The Clerk
memes.description: A newspaper office clerk in Hunger who serves as gatekeeper to publication; represents the institutional barriers the narrator faces in his literary aspirations.

memes.label: The Commandor
memes.description: Newspaper editor referred to by the narrator as "the Commandor," who shows kindness by giving the narrator money and encouragement.

memes.label: The Constable
memes.description: A recurring police constable in Christiania who represents the impersonal authority of the city throughout the narrator's ordeal.

memes.label: The Copégoro
memes.description: A Russian barque moored in Christiania harbor that appears in the climactic final section of Hunger, symbolizing the narrator's escape.

memes.label: The Dampkökken
memes.description: A famous cheap steam cooking-kitchen and eating-house in Christiania, where working people buy warm meals.

memes.label: The Docks
memes.description: "The harbour and dock area of Kristiania, a liminal space in Hunger associated with departure, escape, and the novel's conclusion."

memes.label: The Doppelgänger Motif
memes.description: "The literary motif of the double or shadow-self; applicable to Hunger's narrator, whose self-observation and identity fragmentation create a split consciousness."

memes.label: "The Duke"
memes.description: "A well-known lady's doctor in Christiania, nicknamed 'The Duke,' who diagnoses the narrator as mad in Part II and is seen escorting Ylajali in Part IV — a dual figure of clinical dismissal and romantic rivalry."

memes.label: "The Earth's Increase"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1917 novel Markens Grøde, published in English as Growth of the Soil — Hamsun himself preferred the literal translation The Earth's Increase, which captures the biblical resonance of the original title"

memes.label: The Editor
memes.description: Unnamed newspaper editor in Christiania who accepts the narrator's story for publication.

memes.label: The Flâneur
memes.description: "The figure of the urban stroller-observer, theorized by Baudelaire and Walter Benjamin; Hunger's narrator is a degraded modernist flâneur, observing Kristiania from the position of hunger rather than leisured detachment."

memes.label: The Fortress
memes.description: The fortress overlooking the harbor of Christiania (Akershus Fortress), a landmark the narrator walks towards in moments of contemplation.

memes.label: The Game of Life
memes.description: A play by Knut Hamsun published in 1896, following his dramatic trilogy and preceding Sunset Glow (1898), representing Hamsun's turn toward theatrical writing in the mid-1890s.

memes.label: The Game of Life
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1896 drama, the second play in the Kareno trilogy, set in the Northland with Ivar Kareno at age 39 as a heaven-storming truth-seeker."

memes.label: The Gateway Yard
memes.description: A transitional space in Christiania — a gateway leading to a courtyard — that the narrator passes through in a moment of disorientation, emblematic of the novel's preoccupation with thresholds between public and private, exterior and interior.

memes.label: The Grocer Christy
memes.description: A grocer at No. 13 Groenlandsleret in Christiania to whom the narrator applies for book-keeping work.

memes.label: The Growth of the Soil
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1917 Nobel Prize-winning epic novel of Norwegian peasant life, representing the culmination of his mature style and his turn toward epic social realism.

memes.label: The Guard-House
memes.description: The overnight jail in Christiania where the narrator of Hunger is detained in Part II, functioning as a space of absolute social degradation and darkly comic self-reflection.

memes.label: The Hack-Driver
memes.description: A drosky driver in Christiania who transports the narrator on a wild, intoxicated ride through the city.

memes.label: The Harbour
memes.description: "The harbour area of Kristiania, distinct from the docks; the liminal zone where the city meets the fjord and ships from distant ports arrive."

memes.label: The Human Condition
memes.description: "The universal aspects of human existence — suffering, mortality, the search for meaning — that Hamsun's intensely subjective novel renders in extreme, concentrated form."

memes.label: The Kareno Trilogy
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's dramatic trilogy centered on the character of Ivar Kareno, a student and thinker, marking Hamsun's turn from the novel to the drama at the peak of his early career.

memes.label: The Künstlerroman
memes.description: "A subgenre of the Bildungsroman focusing on the development of an artist; Hunger is a modernist variation of this genre, tracing a writer's formation through deprivation rather than cultivation."

memes.label: The Lame Old Man
memes.description: "A crippled elderly man the narrator encounters and, in a moment of impulsive generosity, gives money to despite his own desperate poverty."

memes.label: The Landlady
memes.description: A recurring figure in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, the landlady of the attic room where the unnamed narrator lives, whose silent demands for rent payments become a source of shame and anxiety for the protagonist.

memes.label: "The Last Joy"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1912 novel, Den sidste Glæde, a transitional work blending novelistic form with uncoordinated philosophical reflections"

memes.label: The Lodgings
memes.description: The temporary lodgings at Tomtegaden No. 11, advertised as "Entertainment and lodgings for travellers," where the narrator finds his third residence in Hunger after the attic episode.

memes.label: The Maid
memes.description: A recurring type of minor character in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, representing the domestic servants and working-class women whom the unnamed narrator encounters in his wanderings through Christiania.

memes.label: The Modernist Novel
memes.description: "The transformation of the novel form in the early 20th century emphasizing subjective consciousness, formal experimentation, and urban experience; Hunger is a foundational text of this tradition."

memes.label: The Narrator
memes.description: The unnamed first-person narrator and protagonist of Knut Hamsun's Hunger — a struggling young writer in Christiania whose experiences of poverty, hunger, and psychological disintegration drive the novel.

memes.label: The Newspaper Office
memes.description: The editorial office of the newspaper in Christiania where the narrator of Hunger submits his articles, a site of creative aspiration, economic hope, and repeated disappointment.

memes.label: "The Norwegian Emigration to America"
memes.description: "The mass emigration of Norwegians to the United States during the 19th century, including Knut Hamsun's own two journeys that shaped his early literary career and critical perspective"

memes.label: The Nun
memes.description: A ship lying alongside the wharf in Christiania, representing the narrator's imagined escape route to Archangel.

memes.label: The Old Man
memes.description: "An unnamed elderly man the narrator encounters in Kristiania; one of the fleeting urban figures who populate Hunger's cityscape."

memes.label: "The Opland Café"
memes.description: "A café in Christiania where the narrator waits to intercept an acquaintance in Part I, located near the road leading to Opland."

memes.label: The Park
memes.description: The park on Castle Hill in Christiania where the narrator of Hunger attempts to write — a space of creative aspiration, public visibility, and recurring failure.

memes.label: The Pawnbroker
memes.description: A pawnbroker on Pyle Street in Christiania with whom the narrator repeatedly transacts to survive.

memes.label: The Prison
memes.description: The Kristiania police station/prison where the narrator of Hunger spends a night after being mistaken for a vagrant; a site of social humiliation and ironic comfort.

memes.label: The Railway Square
memes.description: The public square outside Christiania's railway station, appearing at key moments in Hunger as a threshold between arrival and departure, movement and stasis, the city and the world beyond.

memes.label: "The Ring Is Closed"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1936 novel, exploring the life of a restless dreamer who abandons ambition for simplicity"

memes.label: The Room
memes.description: The narrator's rented room in Christiania — the primary interior space of Hunger where isolation, writing, and the physical experience of starvation converge.

memes.label: The Sign of the Cross
memes.description: A one-act medieval drama that the narrator claims to be writing in Part IV of Hunger; a fiction within the fiction.

memes.label: "The Spiritual Life of Modern America"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1889 book of cultural criticism about the United States, described as 'a masterpiece of distorted criticism' — the product of his two extended stays in America and his failure to connect with the new world"

memes.label: "The Uncle"
memes.description: A pawnbroker in Christiania, referred to by the unnamed narrator as "Uncle," who serves as a recurring figure of material transaction and subtle mockery in Knut Hamsun's Hunger.
aliases: the old man at the desk

memes.label: The Unnamed Narrator
memes.description: The unnamed first-person narrator and protagonist of Knut Hamsun's Hunger, a struggling young writer in Christiania whose experiences of poverty, hunger, and psychological turmoil form the substance of the novel.

memes.label: The Wharf
memes.description: The waterfront at Christiania's harbor — the city's edge — where the narrator of Hunger walks at moments of crisis, contemplating departure and the boundary between life and death.

memes.label: The Wild Choir
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1904 collection of poems, his only volume of verse mentioned in the preface to Hunger, representing a brief excursion into lyric poetry within a career dominated by prose fiction and drama.

memes.label: "The Women at the Pump"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1920 novel, a satirical portrait of small-town Norwegian life in a coastal community"

memes.label: Tivoli
memes.description: The Tivoli amusement park and gardens in Christiania, site of a key assault scene and a recurring reference point in Hunger.

memes.label: Tomtegaden
memes.description: A street in Christiania where the narrator of Hunger finds lodgings in Part III, representing a temporary respite in his cycle of destitution and eviction.

memes.label: Trondheim
memes.description: "Historic Norwegian city, former capital; part of the broader Norwegian geography that frames Hunger's Kristiania-centered world."

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: The concept of truth in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, which the unnamed narrator both seeks and evades — a tension that structures his self-narration and his relationship to his own suffering.

memes.label: University of Christiania
memes.description: The University of Christiania (now University of Oslo), founded in 1811, was Norway's primary institution of higher learning during the period of the novel.

memes.label: University Street
memes.description: A street in Christiania connecting Carl Johann Street to St. Olav's Place — the route along which the narrator escorts Ylajali and a recurring pathway in his urban wandering.

memes.label: Vaterland
memes.description: A working-class district of Christiania where the narrator lodges, known for its poverty and proximity to the railway and harbor.

memes.label: Vaterland Church
memes.description: A church in the Vaterland district of Kristiania; referenced in Hunger as part of the impoverished urban landscape the narrator traverses.

memes.label: "Vendt the Monk"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1903 verse drama in eight acts, centred on a vagabond hero often compared to Peer Gynt"

memes.label: "Vertigo"
memes.description: "The sensation of dizziness and disorientation experienced by the narrator as a physical consequence of prolonged starvation in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Victoria
memes.description: Knut Hamsun's 1898 novel — a love story described in the preface to Hunger as embodying "the tenderest note in his production."

memes.label: "Vognmansgaden"
memes.description: "A street in the Vaterland district of Christiania, connected to the narrator's delusional encounter with Kierulf."

memes.label: "Wayfarers"
memes.description: "Knut Hamsun's 1927 novel, the first of the August trilogy, following the wanderer August"

memes.label: "Weakness"
memes.description: "The narrator's experience of physical and mental debility in Knut Hamsun's Hunger — a state of prostration that is both a symptom of starvation and a source of self-loathing, yet also paradoxically a condition that generates the novel's most acute psychological perceptions"

memes.label: Works by Year
memes.description: Chronological index of works, presenting the literary and publishing timeline of Hamsun's career in context with the world of Hunger.

memes.label: Index of Works
memes.description: Alphabetical index of all literary works and publications referenced in Hunger and related Hamsun scholarship.

memes.label: "Writing"
memes.description: "The narrator's profession and creative practice, representing both his means of survival and his artistic identity in Knut Hamsun's Hunger"

memes.label: Ylajali
memes.description: A mysterious young woman encountered by the unnamed narrator in Knut Hamsun's Hunger, who becomes an object of fascination, fantasy, and romantic idealization across the novel.

memes.label: Youngstorget
memes.description: "A square in central Kristiania (Oslo), originally a market square; referenced in Hunger as part of the narrator's urban geography."
